questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector to create 2A, then enter the resulting vector.
questions.hint: 2A multiplies each component of A by 2. Drag the head outward until c = 2 and read off ⟨2·1, 2·2⟩.
questions.explanation: 2 · ⟨1, 2⟩ = ⟨2·1, 2·2⟩ = ⟨2, 4⟩. Multiplying by a positive scalar stretches the length while the direction stays the same.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches ⟨−3, −6⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: ⟨−3, −6⟩ points opposite to A and is three times as long, so c must be negative. What number times 1 gives −3?
questions.explanation: c · ⟨1, 2⟩ = ⟨−3, −6⟩ means 1c = −3 and 2c = −6, so c = −3. The negative scalar flips the direction and |−3| = 3 triples the length.

questions.prompt: Which of the following could be written as cA? Select all that apply.
questions.hint: A vector is cA only if BOTH components are the same multiple of A. Since A = ⟨2, 3⟩, cA = ⟨2c, 3c⟩.
questions.explanation: ⟨2c, 3c⟩ gives ⟨4, 6⟩ (c = 2), ⟨−2, −3⟩ (c = −1), and ⟨6, 9⟩ (c = 3). The others change the ratio between the components, so they can't be written as cA.
